title: The Job Interview

jokes.text: I've actually been bench pressing 100-pound tortoises because there's no gym here.
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis are having a video call after a long separation; she comments on how good he looks.
jokes.punchline: He reveals his workout routine involves bench-pressing large reptiles as a substitute for gym equipment.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly character-integrated — Ted is a marine biologist/wildlife researcher in the Galapagos, so improvising gym equipment from local fauna is entirely plausible and specific to him. The absurdity is grounded, not random. Great economy: one sentence paints a vivid, funny image.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I've discovered that flies can taste with their feet.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, that's so hot.

jokes.text: Ted! Stop making me jealous of turtles.
jokes.setup: Ted mentions bench-pressing tortoises, implying physical proximity to animals Alexis is now imagining getting attention she wants.
jokes.punchline: Alexis expresses jealousy not of another person but of the tortoises getting to be near Ted.
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable and efficiently constructed. Alexis's brand of self-absorbed romanticism — being jealous of a reptile — is perfectly in character and delivered in five words. The absurdist twist (jealous of turtles, not a person) combined with the sweetness of missing Ted makes this land harder than the setup that preceded it. One of the episode's most memorable lines.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm using endangered animals as gym equipment. I do squats with baby pandas, and I use a slow loris for my bicep curls.

jokes.text: Tortoises, but I do have tur-tell you...
jokes.setup: Alexis uses the word 'turtles' incorrectly; Ted corrects her and pivots to a pun.
jokes.punchline: 'tur-tell you' — a pun on 'turtle' and 'to tell you,' delivered mid-romantic tension.
jokes.explanation: Ted's nature-pun habit is an established character trait, so this is well-integrated. The pun itself is groan-worthy in an intentional, endearing way. Works partly because we know Ted does this. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Actually, it's a tortoise, not a turtle.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You could say I'm really coming out of my shell with these facts!

jokes.text: David walks in on Alexis mid-video-call flirtation and reacts with visible disgust.
jokes.setup: Alexis and Ted are having an intimate moment on a video call; David barges in.
jokes.punchline: David's immediate revulsion ('Is he dressed?') and attempted retreat.
jokes.explanation: A reliable sitcom beat — sibling walks in on intimate moment — elevated by David's specific disgust and the 'Is he dressed?' line revealing he's seen enough of Ted on video calls to have concerns. Competent cringe.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Please tell me you're wearing pants.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Hi, David!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He is.

jokes.text: Johnny casually informs everyone that Ted and Alexis were 'about to engage in cyber-sex'
jokes.setup: David has just barged in; Johnny follows and cheerfully summarizes the situation to the room
jokes.punchline: Johnny's completely matter-of-fact announcement of 'cyber-sex' — a clinical term delivered without any embarrassment — is funnier than any euphemism would be
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in Johnny's delivery and word choice — 'cyber-sex' said with the tone of announcing a dentist appointment. Highly character-specific: only Johnny would be this obtuse and guileless about it. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ted and Alexis were just making out.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Hello, Mr. Rose.

jokes.text: You might wanna say hi to Ted. He and Alexis were about to engage in cyber-sex.
jokes.setup: Johnny enters, David has just walked in on Alexis's video call.
jokes.punchline: David announces it to their father with complete matter-of-fact bluntness.
jokes.explanation: David's gleeful willingness to embarrass his sister in front of their father is perfectly in character. The deadpan delivery of 'cyber-sex' to a parent lands well. Good economy — no hedging, just the bluntest possible word choice.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, the internet is a dangerous place. You have to be very careful about what you post online. People can see it, they can screenshot it, they can use it against you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, my only concern is that my lighting is absolutely dreadful in that photo.

jokes.text: Alexis, what have I told you about putting your body on the internet? Never! Never without proper lighting.
jokes.setup: Johnny (or Moira) is alarmed by the revelation that Alexis was about to engage in cyber-sex.
jokes.punchline: The parental concern isn't about the act itself but about the lighting — revealing Moira's true priorities.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional Moira character comedy. The joke has a perfect two-beat structure: the setup suggests a normal parental objection ('never put your body on the internet'), then the punchline reveals the real concern is aesthetic, not moral. Only Moira Rose could deliver this. Highly quotable, character-specific, and the economy is perfect — 'never without proper lighting' does enormous work in four words. Justified 8.5.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Can everyone please leave? I need some privacy for this call.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I need you all to get out so I can take this long and hard.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: Everybody out so I can finish off with Ted!
jokes.setup: The Rose family has crowded into Alexis's room during her anniversary video call.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's phrasing of 'finish off with Ted' reads as sexually suggestive while she clearly means 'finish talking to Ted.'
jokes.explanation: A classic double-entendre. Works partly because David's 'Sick!' reaction sells it. The joke is functional but not particularly inventive — unintentional sexual phrasing is a standard sitcom tool. The sibling reaction elevates it slightly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was flying Air Français the other day, and I thought, 'How wonderfully sophisticated.'

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's Air Canada.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I was being generous with the pronunciation.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Also, it's named after a guy named Larry.

jokes.text: You stopped for coffee? And a breakfast burrito that I got to go.
jokes.setup: Stevie claims it was an emergency and David came straight there.
jokes.punchline: David admits he stopped for coffee and a breakfast burrito, undermining the urgency of the supposed emergency.
jokes.explanation: The 'got to go' addendum is the key — it shows David thought ahead enough to get takeout on the way to an 'emergency.' The mundane specificity (breakfast burrito, not just coffee) is good character work. Classic David self-absorption played deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So your dad went from owning a deli to owning an airline?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Just like that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, not just like that. There were several years in between.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What happened in those years?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He owned other things.

jokes.text: I need you to help me put together an outfit. I have a job interview this afternoon.
jokes.setup: Stevie says she has an emergency and David rushed over.
jokes.punchline: The 'emergency' is wardrobe help for a job interview — a deliberate anti-climax.
jokes.explanation: The anti-climax of the 'emergency' is well-structured and reveals Stevie's character — she genuinely doesn't have a framework for fashion, so she treats it as crisis-level. Functional misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would make an excellent flight attendant. I have impeccable timing, grace under pressure, and I look phenomenal in a uniform.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What's your experience?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I've flown first class extensively.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's not experience being a flight attendant, that's experience being a passenger.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but I was a very demanding passenger, so I observed their technique closely.

jokes.text: 'Larrierre' sounds like a dollar-store perfume.
jokes.setup: Stevie mentions the airline 'Larry Air' and David mishears or mispronounces it as 'Larrierre.'
jokes.punchline: David's immediate association is that it sounds like a cheap knock-off fragrance.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly David — his first instinct is aesthetic classification, and 'dollar-store perfume' is an instantly funny and specific image. The French-ification of 'Larry Air' into 'Larrierre' is a nice character beat showing his snobbery. Concise and sharp.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have extensive experience in hospitality. I once arranged for a private jet to be stocked with only white truffles and Evian water that had been blessed by a shaman in Iceland.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I also coordinated a dinner service at 35,000 feet where every course was a different shade of beige, which, as you know, is the only acceptable color palette for in-flight dining.

jokes.text: Sandy, the hostess on our private plane. She once let me debone a branzino during turbulence.
jokes.setup: David claims he was told he'd make an excellent flight attendant.
jokes.punchline: The credential he offers is being allowed to debone a fish mid-turbulence, which is both absurdly specific and reveals his oblivious privilege.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent character comedy. David citing a private-plane hostess as a professional reference, and the specific detail of deboning a branzino during turbulence, is both absurd and completely true to his background. The joke works on two levels: his oblivious entitlement AND the genuinely funny image of someone carefully deboning a fish while a plane shakes. 'Branzino' instead of just 'fish' is perfect economy — one word tells you everything about David Rose. Justified 8.0.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm obviously going to get this job. I mean, look at me.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm going to get this job.

jokes.text: She once let me debone a branzino during turbulence.
jokes.setup: David is establishing his flight attendant credentials.
jokes.punchline: He cites deboning a branzino during turbulence as evidence of his suitability for the job — a deeply Rose-family logic.
jokes.explanation: Specific, absurd, and perfectly character-integrated. Only David Rose would interpret deboning a fish as a passenger as qualification for working on a plane. 'Branzino during turbulence' is a great image. Sets up a callback later.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: So you're telling me the motel is called the Rosebud?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: And you own it?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I do.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: A man owning a motel called the Rosebud?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it came with the name. I'm not going to change it just because it has a feminine quality to it. That would be small-minded.

jokes.text: No. I just think if it's between the two of us, we all know who's getting the job.
jokes.setup: David says he was told he'd make an excellent flight attendant; Stevie asks by whom.
jokes.punchline: David implies he's clearly the superior candidate, which the audience knows is absurd given Stevie is the one with the interview.
jokes.explanation: David's competitive delusion is consistent and funny. Works as character comedy — his self-confidence is completely unfounded and the audience knows it.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know what, Johnny? I'm gonna help you with the bank. I'll tell 'em about all your successful business ventures.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Like that time you invested in that... what was it... that thing that went belly up in three weeks?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, that's not helping.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no, I got this. Or how about that other business idea that lost you half a million dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Okay, stop.

jokes.text: Well, if this is what we're working with, I think we can hand me the contract today.
jokes.setup: David has been helping Stevie get dressed; he now surveys his own outfit.
jokes.punchline: David's narcissism pivots to confidence about himself rather than helping Stevie.
jokes.explanation: A solid character beat showing David's reflex self-promotion. The phrasing 'hand me the contract' is confident and funny. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Look, I'm a very important man in this town. I sit on the town council, I'm a member of the Rotary Club, and I know the mayor personally.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: That's nice, but it doesn't change your credit score.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, what if I told you my son is dating your daughter?

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: I don't have a daughter.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What if you did? Think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: I'm going to have to ask you to leave.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Okay, okay. One more thing. How much would it take for this to go away? Hypothetically.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: That's called bribery.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't say it had to be money.

jokes.text: Sorry, I might've gotten confused there. The bank has assessed that you don't have enough assets to secure the loan.
jokes.setup: The manager says the proposal is 'excellent' and Johnny celebrates.
jokes.punchline: The immediate reversal — 'unfortunately, we couldn't offer the loan' — turns the praise into a comedic gut-punch.
jokes.explanation: Classic setup-subversion structure — the compliment raises hopes only to immediately dash them. The manager's 'sorry, I might've gotten confused there' is a funny acknowledgment of his own misleading framing. Functional joke.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I have influence in this town. People respect me.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, last week you tried to get a discount at the gas station and they laughed you out.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That was different. I wasn't trying hard enough. This time I'm going to show them what real power looks like.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. You're right. I have no influence. Can we just go home?

jokes.text: So, you're Stevie Budd? No. Believe it or not, that's actually a woman's name.
jokes.setup: The bank manager notes the motel is in Johnny's partner's name, not his.
jokes.punchline: When asked if he's Stevie Budd, Johnny deadpans that it's 'actually a woman's name' — a mild, understated corrective.
jokes.explanation: 'Believe it or not' carries the weight of the joke — Johnny's weary exasperation at having to explain this. Competent deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Did you know that a testudine is a fascinating creature?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, a what? Is that like a disease?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, darling. It's a turtle.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A turtle? Why would you say testudine? Just say turtle!

jokes.text: I did not wanna play this card, but... I am the mayor. You already mentioned that.
jokes.setup: Roland dramatically announces he's about to play his trump card.
jokes.punchline: His big card is being the mayor — which he'd already mentioned, making it worthless.
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent: the theatrical build-up ('I did not wanna play this card') followed by the anticlimactic reveal of a card that's already been played. The manager's flat 'You already mentioned that' is pitch-perfect deadpan. Good economy and strong character integration — Roland's delusion about his own importance is a recurring source of comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Turtles are terrible pets. They live for decades, they carry salmonella, and quite frankly, holding one feels like you're cradling a raw chicken cutlet.

jokes.text: Did I mention I went steady with your mom? Unfortunately, you did.
jokes.setup: Roland has already played his 'mayor' card to no effect; he tries another.
jokes.punchline: His next gambit — a past relationship with the banker's mother — has also already been mentioned, equally failing to impress.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'I am the mayor' to 'I went steady with your mom' is a wonderful absurdist escalation. Both reveals have the same deflating structure (already mentioned), which creates a comedic pattern. The 'unfortunately, you did' response implies the manager is embarrassed by this fact. The escalation of desperation is character-perfect for Roland. This is the episode's best Roland joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm concerned about the turtle's health. These creatures can carry all sorts of diseases.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Just wash your hands. I wash my hands like, all the time, and I'm fine.

jokes.text: Okay, then I'm out. I got nothing.
jokes.setup: Both of Roland's influence cards have been rejected.
jokes.punchline: He folds with complete abruptness — 'I got nothing' — rather than continuing to try.
jokes.explanation: The button on the Roland-at-the-bank sequence. 'I got nothing' is the exact right words, with perfect economy. The utter lack of dignity or persistence is very Roland. Works as a satisfying button to the preceding escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: She thinks naming a pet after her boyfriend counts as a 'shared experience'

jokes.text: Please tell me it's not a testudine. No, it's a turtle.
jokes.setup: Moira sees something Alexis is carrying and immediately uses a scientific classification term.
jokes.punchline: Alexis has no idea what a testudine is and just says 'it's a turtle' — which is exactly what Moira feared.
jokes.explanation: Moira using the taxonomic term 'testudine' while hoping it's NOT one, only for Alexis to casually confirm it is (without even knowing the word), is a lovely two-character joke. It captures both characters perfectly: Moira's theatrical vocabulary and Alexis's blissful obliviousness. The structure — question in elevated language, answer in the simplest terms — is elegant.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Marriage is like a film trilogy. The first one is passionate and unexpected, the second one is slightly disappointing, and by the third one, you're just hoping it ends.

jokes.text: Turtles do not pets make.
jokes.setup: Alexis has brought home a turtle named Ted.
jokes.punchline: Moira's objection is delivered in inverted syntax ('pets make' instead of 'make pets') — a Yoda-esque construction that is pure Moira Rose.
jokes.explanation: The inverted syntax is Moira's distinctive verbal signature — she speaks in a slightly scrambled, theatrical register. This is a perfect example: the content is normal (turtles aren't good pets) but the delivery is completely her own. High character integration score is justified.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's a very... conservative outfit choice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's funny that you would say that, because this is actually a very well-tailored, expensive piece of clothing.

jokes.text: The poor things are riddled with a myriad of diseases. You may as well tie a leash around a raw chicken cutlet.
jokes.setup: Moira warns Alexis about the health dangers of keeping a turtle.
jokes.punchline: The comparison to a 'raw chicken cutlet' on a leash — a vivid, absurd image — elevates a standard health warning into something uniquely Moira.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's strongest lines. The image of tying a leash around a raw chicken cutlet is both viscerally funny and logically sound as a metaphor (disease vector, equally impractical as a pet). Only Moira Rose could produce this comparison. The rhythm of the sentence is perfect — the build through 'myriad of diseases' to the absurd concrete image. Highly quotable. Justified 8.4.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's a good joke. But I want to be clear, I wasn't dressing like a youth pastor. I was dressing like someone who has a very nice life and a great sense of style.

jokes.text: Relax, they said he's already been washed.
jokes.setup: Moira has just described turtles as disease-ridden health hazards.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's only counter is that 'he's already been washed' — treating this like a sufficient rebuttal to salmonella concerns.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's breezy dismissal of medical concerns with a single bath is perfectly in character — her logic is always just slightly off. The economy is great; one line does all the work. Works as a good follow to Moira's elaborate warning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, they've added a phone in the room. How luxurious. I suppose next they'll be installing indoor plumbing and electricity as standard features.

jokes.text: If you desire a love with longevity, the key is to have as little in common as possible.
jokes.setup: Moira is offering relationship advice to Alexis about surviving long distance.
jokes.punchline: Her wisdom is the direct opposite of conventional relationship advice — 'as little in common as possible' — and she delivers it with absolute conviction.
jokes.explanation: Moira's relationship advice being the precise inverse of conventional wisdom is character-perfect — she and Johnny ARE quite different people, so from her perspective this is legitimate observation. The joke works on multiple levels: it's absurd advice, it's ironically true for her marriage, and it's delivered with total sincerity. Well-earned.

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: So, I see here you have some legal troubles on your record...

dialogue.speaker: Applicant
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it was all a misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: It says here you were arrested for fraud, embezzlement, and tax evasion.

dialogue.speaker: Applicant
dialogue.line: Like I said, misunderstandings.

jokes.text: After an award-worthy trilogy of decades together, your father and I still astonish each other.
jokes.setup: Moira is expounding on the secret to her marriage's longevity.
jokes.punchline: 'Award-worthy trilogy of decades' — describing their 30+ year marriage as if it were a film franchise she starred in.
jokes.explanation: Moira framing her own marriage in cinematic terms ('award-worthy trilogy') is deeply in character for a former soap opera actress who views her life as performance. Solid character joke.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm a perfectionist. I'm too detail-oriented. I'm not a great delegator. I can be impatient. I'm not always the best communicator. I second-guess myself sometimes. I can be a little stubborn. I'm not great with change. I tend to hold grudges. I'm not always—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yeah, no, I think we've got it.

jokes.text: Although today, I will but feign surprise when he tells me he's secured a hefty loan to initiate our imminent connecting departure from this lengthy layover.
jokes.setup: Moira is confidently predicting Johnny will secure the bank loan.
jokes.punchline: The elaborate theatrical phrasing ('feign surprise,' 'imminent connecting departure,' 'lengthy layover') to describe something as simple as 'we're leaving this town' is the joke — and of course the audience knows Johnny has just failed at the bank.
jokes.explanation: Works on dramatic irony — we've just seen Johnny fail at the bank. Moira's confidence is misplaced and her theatrical vocabulary (calling living in Schitt's Creek 'a lengthy layover') is amusingly grandiose. The joke has two layers: Moira's language and the dramatic irony of her false assumption.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: So, David, what would you say is your greatest weakness?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'd have to say I'm a perfectionist. I care too much about my work, and I hold myself to impossibly high standards.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: I see. And is that actually a weakness, or are you just describing a strength?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I... well, it can be both.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: No, it really can't.

jokes.text: No. I have to give Ted his pellets, and then put him down for a little nap. Sexy.
jokes.setup: Moira asks Alexis if she'll be joining the lunch celebration.
jokes.punchline: Alexis declines to care for the turtle Ted, and Moira's deadpan 'Sexy' in response to this information is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Moira's single-word 'Sexy' as a response to turtle care duties is perfectly timed and deeply sarcastic. The joke is in the contrast: Alexis's very unsexy afternoon of turtle husbandry vs. Moira's withering label for it. One word doing maximum work.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, every time you open your mouth, it's like you're doing us all a favor by sharing your thoughts. But the truth is, you're just making it worse.

jokes.text: Uh, you dress for the job you want. So you wanna be a youth pastor?
jokes.setup: David has shown up in an outfit he thinks is professional (actually Patrick's clothes).
jokes.punchline: Stevie asks if he wants to be a youth pastor — implying his outfit is hopelessly square.
jokes.explanation: The 'youth pastor' comparison is specific and funny — not just 'you look uncool' but a precise, evocative image. It's also a good character moment for Stevie, whose own fashion sense is non-existent, making her the last person who should be judging. The structure is tight: classic 'dress for the job' saying → subverted specific punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Now, I probably shouldn't be telling you this, but Bob's finances are in worse shape than his hairline.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And between you and me, those hair plugs cost him forty grand. But obviously, I'm very discreet about these things.

jokes.text: Hi. Wow. While that joke was surprisingly sharp, these are Patrick's clothes. So, you might wanna tell that to my fiancé's face.
jokes.setup: Stevie has mocked David's outfit as 'youth pastor.'
jokes.punchline: David acknowledges the quality of the joke ('surprisingly sharp') before revealing the clothes are Patrick's — redirecting the insult to his fiancé.
jokes.explanation: David's genuine, slightly begrudging compliment to Stevie for making a good joke is a lovely moment — meta-comedic without being irritating. The pivot to 'tell that to my fiancé's face' deflects the embarrassment onto Patrick in a way that's both funny and affectionate. Good structure and character work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Bob, darling, I see you've decided to drape yourself in the skin of a deceased bovine. How very... tactile of you.

jokes.text: 'Larry Air, recent improvements: all chairs will have tray tables, not just first class.' I feel like I'm flying on the Concorde.
jokes.setup: David reads aloud from Larry Air's promotional material at the open call.
jokes.punchline: The 'improvement' of adding tray tables to all seats is so minimal that David sarcastically compares it to the legendary supersonic luxury aircraft.
jokes.explanation: The Concorde reference is well-chosen — it's specifically luxurious and slightly anachronistic, which is very David. Reading the promotional copy aloud as criticism while in the middle of an open call application is good character comedy. The joke establishes the running gag of Larry Air being extremely budget.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I need to use the bathroom. These leather pants are cutting off my circulation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's... more information than I needed.

jokes.text: I should mention, if anyone here is involved in the class-action lawsuit against Larry Air, you are disqualified from applying.
jokes.setup: Carol opens the group interview enthusiastically, welcoming the applicants.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that there's an active class-action lawsuit against the airline — mentioned casually as a disqualifier — tells us everything about the quality of this carrier.
jokes.explanation: This is a well-constructed reveal. The class-action lawsuit mention is dropped so casually, as if it's routine HR procedure, that the absurdity is all in the delivery and context. It's dark comedy done right — the airline is terrible, and everyone in the room is still applying. Good structure and strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, well, well. Looks like we've got ourselves a biter on the line. This investor's gonna be easy to reel in.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I can already sea the dollar signs. We're gonna have a real catch of a deal here.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This one's gonna be a keeper, mark my words.

jokes.text: Stevie's interview answer: 'I'm disorganized... and indecisive... I also...' 'Okay, we just needed one.'
jokes.setup: Carol asks each applicant to state their biggest weakness; Stevie goes first.
jokes.punchline: Stevie lists multiple weaknesses enthusiastically — 'disorganized... indecisive... I also...' — and Carol has to cut her off: 'We just needed one.'
jokes.explanation: Classic Stevie — she's refreshingly, disastrously honest in interview contexts, volunteering weaknesses where others would give faux-humble answers. The increasing alarm from the interviewer ('yes... okay...') before the cutoff is a well-structured escalation. The joke works because it inverts the typical interview trope (hiding weaknesses) and reveals genuine character — Stevie doesn't know how to perform for others.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I think it was... it was right after David and I broke up. Or maybe right before? No, it was definitely around the same time as when the turtle got out.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Actually, now that I think about it, I'm not entirely sure which happened first — David leaving or the turtle escaping. They kind of blur together in my mind.

jokes.text: Hi. David Rose. Would we consider loyalty a weakness? If not, I'm also really honest. I'm just gonna put down chatty.
jokes.setup: Carol asks David his biggest weakness at the group interview.
jokes.punchline: David tries to spin a positive trait as a weakness ('loyalty'), gets called out (implicitly), offers another non-weakness ('honest'), and the interviewer just decides to write 'chatty' for him.
jokes.explanation: A perfect contrast to Stevie's answer. Where Stevie over-shares real weaknesses, David gives the textbook (and clichéd) job-interview non-answer ('loyalty as a weakness'). Carol's decision to just write 'chatty' is the deadpan button that rewards the contrast. Good structure, well-integrated character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Wait, which Ted are we talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, the Ted that I'm dating? There's only one Ted in my life, Twyla.

jokes.text: Well, she said apparently Johnny doesn't have enough assets. / Yeah, and the problem is, how do you get assets without the loan? / Roland, you're not adding anything to the sentence, you're just repeating what I said.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Roland discuss the failed bank loan; Roland echoes everything Johnny says.
jokes.punchline: Johnny calls Roland out on literally just repeating his sentences back to him, contributing nothing.
jokes.explanation: Roland's echo-chamber conversation style is a reliable character bit. Johnny's exasperated meta-observation ('you're just repeating what I said') is the right way to button this. Works as character comedy and the explicit naming of the bit adds a self-aware layer.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just realized I lost a turtle. Like, I had a living creature in my care and I completely forgot about it. What does that say about me?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I can't even keep a pet alive. How am I supposed to keep myself together?

jokes.text: Well, you know, I don't wanna be the town gossip here, but... Bob's financial statement was on his desk, and that garage is doing great! Plus, he got plugs last year, but you didn't hear that from me.
jokes.setup: Moira suggests finding a local investor with money; Roland claims not to want to gossip.
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately gossips at length — revealing Bob's finances AND his hair plugs — while maintaining the fiction of not being a gossip.
jokes.explanation: The classic 'I don't want to gossip BUT...' setup is executed efficiently. The inclusion of the hair plugs alongside the financial information — as if equally relevant — is the right comedic escalation. 'You didn't hear that from me' after volunteering all of it is a good button.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh, that happened to me once! Well, not exactly like that, but similar. I was at the food court, and I sat down at what I thought was my table, but it turned out to be someone else's table.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: And then security came, and they thought I was stealing french fries, so they tackled me.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Turns out it was the mayor's table, and I landed on his wife, and she went into early labor.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: The baby was fine, but the mayor banned me from the mall for life. And also from the hospital. And also I'm not allowed to be within 100 yards of his family.

jokes.text: Oh, look at you, Robert, with the hides of a herd of Holstein on your back.
jokes.setup: Bob arrives at the restaurant wearing what is evidently a large leather jacket or outfit.
jokes.punchline: Moira describes it as 'the hides of a herd of Holstein' — a characteristically elaborate way to say he's wearing a lot of cow leather.
jokes.explanation: Moira at her best — a genuine compliment that's actually an insult, delivered with theatrical enthusiasm. 'Hides of a herd of Holstein' is specific, alliterative, and paints a vivid image of an enormous, excessive leather garment. Only Moira would phrase it this way, and it works as both flattery (she sounds impressed) and mockery. Highly quotable. Justified 8.0.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Twyla, can you just keep an eye out for the turtle?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: A turtle? In the kitchen?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, it's fine. Just don't let it get near the walk-in or anything.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Okay... I'll just add that to my list of things to worry about.

jokes.text: I just had two cups of coffee. I don't wanna push my luck. I mean, these pants, they take like a year to unlace, so...
jokes.setup: Bob says he doesn't have time to chat and is in a hurry.
jokes.punchline: The reason for his urgency: coffee-induced bathroom pressure, and his elaborate pants make bathroom visits time-consuming.
jokes.explanation: Bob's bathroom urgency connecting back to Moira's comment about his elaborate outfit is a nice structural callback. The detail that his pants 'take like a year to unlace' is specific and visual. Functional comedy that also lands a visual joke about what he's wearing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, but I've already been upgraded thrice this month. I don't think that's physically possible.

jokes.text: Rose's, I hope you like fish, 'cause I think we just reeled in a big one.
jokes.setup: Roland watches Bob rush past, having overheard the investment pitch setup.
jokes.punchline: Roland uses a fishing metaphor — completely unsubtle — about their plan to get Bob to invest.
jokes.explanation: A fairly standard fishing metaphor. Not particularly clever or character-specific. Works as a transition beat more than a strong joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, have you tried giving the baby a sedative? Or perhaps just opening the emergency exit?

jokes.text: Hey, Alexis. Another green smoothie, no greens?
jokes.setup: Alexis visits the café.
jokes.punchline: Twyla's casual notation of Alexis's standing order — a 'green smoothie' that contains no actual greens — tells us everything about Alexis.
jokes.explanation: A single line that efficiently characterizes both Alexis (health-conscious aesthetic, not substance) and Twyla (cheerfully matter-of-fact about the absurdity). Good economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: David, you don't understand airline policy at all.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I—

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: No, no, no. *snap snap* Good effort though.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, are you snapping at me like I'm a golden retriever?

jokes.text: No, it's nothing. It's just a turtle. His name is Ted and he's gone. And I can't remember where I put him, and I thought it was in the motel, but he could've like crawled out of my purse...
jokes.setup: Alexis asks if she left something behind; Twyla asks what she lost.
jokes.punchline: The casual escalation from 'it's nothing' to 'it's a turtle named after my boyfriend and he crawled out of my purse' is a perfect Alexis moment.
jokes.explanation: The structure of 'it's nothing / it's actually this very specific absurd thing' is well-executed. The detail of the turtle crawling out of her purse implies she was carrying it around town in her handbag, which is funnier than the dialogue states. Good Alexis characterization.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, they called my name!

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: David Rose, you're safe.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know, I heard—wait, what?

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: You're eliminated.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But you just said I was safe!

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: No, I said you're safe to leave. You're out.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, this is humiliating.

jokes.text: The turtle flew to the Galapagos? No, Ted the turtle is missing, I am now talking about my boyfriend Ted, Twy. It'd be great if you could keep up.
jokes.setup: Alexis confusingly refers to both the turtle Ted and her boyfriend Ted in quick succession.
jokes.punchline: Twyla briefly thinks the turtle flew to the Galapagos; Alexis impatiently corrects her for not tracking two separate Teds.
jokes.explanation: The dual-Ted confusion is mined perfectly here. Twyla's literal interpretation ('the turtle flew to the Galapagos') is a great moment of innocent absurdism. Alexis's 'it'd be great if you could keep up' while being the person who created the confusion is perfect character irony.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, he said I didn't meet his standards. Which is rich coming from a man who once told me his only qualification was that he could open a door.

jokes.text: The slowest animal on earth escaped from me. That can't not be a bad sign.
jokes.setup: Alexis bemoans losing the turtle Ted.
jokes.punchline: She correctly identifies the absurdity — that the slowest animal on Earth escaped from her — and reads it as an omen.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. The logic is airtight — losing a turtle IS objectively embarrassing and somewhat inexplicable given how slow they are. The double-negative 'can't not be a bad sign' adds a layer of Alexis verbal tic. Highly quotable, perfectly economical, and funnier on reflection. Justified 8.2.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Look, I understand your concerns, but if you think about it logically, we're actually better off staying together as a group.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because if one of us leaves, we're no longer a complete group, which means we've all failed individually.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So really, the only way for any of us to succeed is if we all stay exactly where we are. It's simple mathematics.

jokes.text: My mom had a turtle dove that was eaten by her ex-fiancé's snake. That was a bad sign. And it happened on Valentine's Day. And her fiancé was a Satanist.
jokes.setup: Alexis laments that losing the turtle must be a bad sign; Twyla offers a comparable story.
jokes.punchline: Twyla's story escalates from turtle dove → eaten by snake → Valentine's Day → fiancé was a Satanist. Each beat is a new, worse detail.
jokes.explanation: This is a Twyla masterpiece. Her stories of family tragedy delivered with complete placidity are one of the show's signature comedy devices. The escalation here is perfectly structured: each sentence adds a worse element, with 'her fiancé was a Satanist' as the perfect kicker. The details are specific (Valentine's Day adds irony, Satanist is the apex absurdity) and the deadpan delivery makes it funnier. This is among the episode's two or three best jokes — Twyla tragedy-escalation at its finest. Justified 8.7.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm taking the peanuts.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: All of them.

jokes.text: And if it makes you feel any better, turtles actually thrive out of captivity.
jokes.setup: Twyla has been trying (and failing) to comfort Alexis about the missing turtle.
jokes.punchline: Her final comfort is that turtles do better without owners — which is not actually reassuring but is delivered as if it is.
jokes.explanation: Twyla's brand of comfort that isn't actually comforting works well here. The implication that the turtle is better off having escaped Alexis is funny and slightly mean, delivered in her characteristic gentle manner.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Bob, those are some really expensive-looking pants. Are those Italian?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They're from a catalogue.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: A very fancy catalogue.

jokes.text: And if you do see Ted like waddling around in the kitchen, just feed him a carrot and shoot me a text. I hope you're joking. Me too, girl. Love you!
jokes.setup: Alexis leaves the café after their conversation about the missing turtle.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's parting instructions are genuine (she really might need this information), Twyla reacts with appropriate alarm, and Alexis confirms she's also alarmed — delivered cheerfully.
jokes.explanation: 'Me too, girl. Love you!' as a button is excellent — it's Alexis being warm and terrified simultaneously, in exactly her register. The 'waddling around in the kitchen' phrasing is specific and creates a funny image. Good scene-closing joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh my God, this is so exciting! A real business investment opportunity!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, we need to think like predators, Jocelyn. We circle. We isolate our prey. We move in for the kill.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You mean Bob?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Exactly. Bob is a wounded gazelle, and we are very hungry lions.

jokes.text: David playing the role of a flight attendant addressing a crying baby: 'This baby behind me has been crying for over an hour and I just can't take it anymore.' 'You and me both.'
jokes.setup: David is performing a role-play exercise in the Larry Air interview, playing a flight attendant.
jokes.punchline: David breaks character almost immediately by sharing his personal feelings about the crying baby rather than addressing the passenger's complaint — empathizing with the complainer instead of de-escalating.
jokes.explanation: David's 'You and me both' — agreeing with the passenger's complaint about the baby instead of de-escalating — is a perfect character moment. He cannot suppress his own opinions even in a job interview exercise. The follow-up offering complimentary alcohol compounds the error perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We're very close with Bill and Melinda Gates. We summer together.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Every summer.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We call them Billy and Mel.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Very casual relationship.

jokes.text: I could either move those two to the back of the plane, or I could offer you some complimentary alcohol to make your flight a little more bearable.
jokes.setup: David is playing a flight attendant; having already broken character, he now proposes solutions.
jokes.punchline: Both solutions violate Larry Air policy ('no seat changes,' 'complimentary is not in our vocabulary') — David has memorized nothing about the airline.
jokes.explanation: Works as a callback to the earlier material about Larry Air's policies — David was just mocking those policies, and now he's proposing the exact things they don't offer. Good structural irony. Carol's dual rebuttal ('no seat changes' and 'complimentary is not in our vocabulary') is a satisfying payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I bought this suit for the dinner because I thought it would impress the clients.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you spent three thousand dollars on a suit?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The dinner was cancelled an hour ago.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: What? No, I... I needed to look successful. I'm a failure, David. The business is failing, my marriage is—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm standing right here, Robert.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: The point is, I thought if I looked the part, maybe I could finally feel like I wasn't letting everyone down.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, we don't care what suit you're wearing.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I do.

jokes.text: But finger snaps for David! We don't... Sure. We don't... Sure.
jokes.setup: Carol has just told David everything he did was wrong in the exercise.
jokes.punchline: Carol offers 'finger snaps' as consolation — a very specific, slightly condescending gesture — and the group uncertainly half-participates.
jokes.explanation: The uncertain group participation ('We don't... Sure') is a good ambient cringe moment. Carol's use of 'finger snaps' as corporate consolation is character-specific and slightly odd. A solid texture joke.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: She left me for the minister. Apparently he's the 'bad boy' type, and religious experience has a whole different meaning.

jokes.text: If I call your name, please stand up. Rupinder Pimms, Tracey Mickleson, Felix Ng, and David Rose. Fuck yes!
jokes.setup: Carol announces who will advance to the final round.
jokes.punchline: David's triumphant 'Fuck yes!' — only for Carol to immediately tell him he's been eliminated.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly executed misdirection. The audience knows David won't get the job; his 'Fuck yes!' makes the reversal funnier by showing how wrong his self-assessment is. The structure (name called → jubilation → 'I regret to inform you') is tight. David's 'This is so embarrassing' before he understands is an extra beat of cringe. High impact — this is a big laugh moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Bob, darling, I heard about your marriage. I'm so sorry. But you know what this means? A fresh start! New beginnings! And more importantly, a chance to liquidate assets.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm thinking we could help him with the real estate side of things. The house sale, the settlement. We'd take a modest commission, of course.

jokes.text: This is so embarrassing. I thought you said David Rose. I did.
jokes.setup: David has just celebrated being called to stand, then been told he's eliminated.
jokes.punchline: David tries to create an out for himself ('I thought you said David Rose'), and Carol confirms she did — leaving him with no escape.
jokes.explanation: Carol's flat 'I did' — without elaboration, explanation, or sympathy — is the perfect deadpan response. It closes David's attempted escape with minimum words and maximum impact. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're being defensive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not being defensive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And I don't care what you think anyway.

jokes.text: Too bad they didn't have a branzino to de-bone because you would've got this.
jokes.setup: David has just been eliminated from the interview.
jokes.punchline: Stevie references David's earlier credential (deboning a branzino on their private plane) as the only thing that might have gotten him the job.
jokes.explanation: An excellent callback. The branzino joke from the preparation scene was funny; this recontextualization is even better. Stevie is simultaneously comforting David and ribbing him — the warmth and the bite coexist perfectly. The callback rewards viewers who caught the earlier joke and adds a layer of earned comedy. 'You would've got this' delivers both as sarcasm and, in Stevie's world, genuine affection. Justified 8.2.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm doing really well, David. Like, really well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I can see that.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Better than you expected?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I wouldn't say that.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Better than you're doing?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie...

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Just say it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fine. Yes. You're doing better than I am. Happy?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Thrilled.

jokes.text: Yeah, hi, Carol? Uh, it's just that some of us drove some of the other applicants here, so it might just be easier if we all just moved forward into the next round.
jokes.setup: David has been told to wait in the hall after being eliminated.
jokes.punchline: David tries to negotiate his reinstatement into the interview with the absurd logic that carpooling entitles him to advance.
jokes.explanation: David's carpool argument is a brilliantly delusional piece of logic — he drove Stevie there, therefore they should both advance. The bureaucratic framing ('it might just be easier if we all...') of his completely invalid request is very David. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm reading a scathing review of Larry Air, and it's absolutely devastating. The reviewer calls their customer service 'aggressively unhelpful' and their planes 'flying coffins.'

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, why are you reading this to me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because you work there, and I'm genuinely embarrassed for you.

jokes.text: I'll be taking some of these complimentary peanuts then. / Oh, as I pointed out, nothing is complimentary on Larry Air. / I'm taking the peanuts, Carol!
jokes.setup: David has been dismissed from the interview and is making his exit.
jokes.punchline: David attempts to take peanuts as compensation; Carol invokes the 'nothing is complimentary' policy; David defiantly takes them anyway.
jokes.explanation: A wonderful callback and running gag payoff. David earlier read about the 'complimentary' policy with disdain; now he's on the wrong side of it. 'I'm taking the peanuts, Carol!' is one of the episode's most quotable lines — the use of her name makes it funnier, the defiance is perfect David, and the petty stakes (airline peanuts) are exactly right. This is excellent comedy. Justified 8.0.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I need you to prove you're not a serial killer. Blink twice if you're a serial killer.

dialogue.speaker: Limo Driver
dialogue.line: I'm not blinking.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly. Serial killers always blink. It's like their tell.

jokes.text: That is one snazzy outfit. Somebody's got some money to burn.
jokes.setup: Roland, Johnny, and Moira are having dinner with Bob to pitch him as an investor; Roland is trying to butter Bob up.
jokes.punchline: Roland's compliment about the 'snazzy outfit' is the setup for the investment trap — he's trying to signal Bob is wealthy. But 'money to burn' is both a compliment and a hint.
jokes.explanation: A functional joke that establishes the scheming dinner dynamic. 'Snazzy' is slightly funny as applied to what is presumably an over-the-top leather ensemble. Not particularly strong.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, you look so handsome and put-together.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Thank you. Well, I should tell you, I'm only dressed from the waist up. I'm wearing sweatpants.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's a video call. No one can see below the desk.

jokes.text: Good, then let's go get our lamb. / Oh, are we having lamb? / No, just my Sloppy Jocelyns.
jokes.setup: Johnny uses a metaphor ('let's go get our lamb') about getting Bob to invest.
jokes.punchline: Roland takes it literally, asking if they're having lamb; Jocelyn clarifies it's 'Sloppy Jocelyns' — her homemade version of Sloppy Joes.
jokes.explanation: Roland's literal interpretation and the Sloppy Jocelyns punchline work well. The name 'Sloppy Jocelyns' is very funny — she's named the dish after herself, which implies pride in something called 'sloppy.' Good character beat for both Roland and Jocelyn.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, he does have very nice hands.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh! Hi.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Hi Ted!

jokes.text: John, were you able to return Bill and Melinda's call today about the hmm hmm-hmm? / Well, you know, Moira, we're not supposed to be discussing investment opportunities at the table, no matter how lucrative they might be. / But you know what the Gateses are like - Bill and Melinda.
jokes.setup: The Roses are trying to make Bob think they have wealthy, famous investor contacts.
jokes.punchline: Moira and Johnny perform an elaborately staged fake conversation about Bill and Melinda Gates, name-dropping repeatedly and unconvincingly.
jokes.explanation: Moira and Johnny performing their old wealth for a mark is a lovely character moment showing how they've adapted their skills. The 'hmm hmm-hmm' as a placeholder for a fake conversation is a funny choice. 'You know what the Gateses are like — Bill and Melinda' is the right comedic peak — the clarification ('Bill and Melinda') as if Bob might know different Gateses.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I named the fly after you because it's beautiful and graceful.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, that fly eats garbage and throws up on its food before it digests it.

jokes.text: Wow! You know Bill Gates? / Oh, who doesn't? But you're right, Bob, why gild their lilies when there might be someone right under our noses looking for a lucrative venture?
jokes.setup: Bob is impressed by the Gates name-drop; Moira immediately pivots to making Bob feel like the real prize.
jokes.punchline: 'Why gild their lilies when there might be someone right under our noses' — Moira's theatrical way of saying 'we'd rather deal with you, Bob from the garage' while pretending it's an upgrade from Bill Gates.
jokes.explanation: Moira's grifter instincts are on full display. The transition from Bill Gates to Bob as an 'upgrade' is the absurdist comedy of their circumstances. 'Gild their lilies' is a nice Moira-ism (the phrase is usually 'gild the lily') — she scrambles idioms slightly.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're opening a new location for the business. It's going to be a major expansion.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's wonderful news, Johnny! Congratulations!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh my goodness, that's so exciting! But tell me—what color are the napkins going to be?

jokes.text: Gwen, she told me, she said, 'I like bad boys,' and now she's holed up with our minister. She was bragging about a religious experience.
jokes.setup: Bob reveals that Gwen has left him and what she said.
jokes.punchline: His wife ran off with the minister, and her description of it as a 'religious experience' takes on an obvious double meaning.
jokes.explanation: Two jokes in one: Gwen calling the minister a 'bad boy' (a category he doesn't typically occupy) and 'religious experience' as double entendre. The minister-as-bad-boy inversion is the stronger element. Darkly funny and well-delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So Johnny comes to me with this 'business opportunity,' right? Says he's got a sure thing. Guaranteed returns.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I'm telling you, this is legitimate.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And I believe him! Why wouldn't I? The man's never lied to me before.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Roland, Johnny lies constantly.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't know that at the time!

jokes.text: Perhaps there's an upside to all this, fiscally speaking.
jokes.setup: Bob has just tearfully revealed his wife left him for their minister.
jokes.punchline: Moira's response is to immediately look for the financial upside of his heartbreak — 'fiscally speaking.'
jokes.explanation: 'Fiscally speaking' is doing tremendous work — it's the most Moira possible response to someone's heartbreak. Her inability to set aside the investment pitch even for a moment of human sympathy is a devastating character observation delivered as comedy. Good economy and very high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Of course the turtle shows up now.

jokes.text: So, are you just never gonna talk to me ever again? / I asked you to roll up the window in the car. The highway smelled like manure.
jokes.setup: After the interview, David is clearly upset; Stevie asks if he's going to stay mad.
jokes.punchline: David's stated grievance is not the humiliation of being rejected but that Stevie wouldn't roll up the car window.
jokes.explanation: David displacing his humiliation onto the car window grievance is both funny and revealing — he's too proud to admit he's upset about the rejection. The manure detail is specific and grounding. Good David character work.

jokes.text: Did Carol embarrass herself by sending me away in front of everybody? / Absolutely. / Was the entire group shocked by that decision? / Seemed like it based on some people's faces.
jokes.setup: David is asking leading questions to make himself feel better about being rejected.
jokes.punchline: Stevie plays along with his delusion — 'absolutely,' 'seemed like it based on some people's faces' — with just enough hedging to be funny.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's patient, affectionate indulgence of David's self-serving narrative is a perfect expression of their friendship. 'Seemed like it based on some people's faces' is beautifully hedged — she's lying to comfort him, but carefully. Good character integration for both.

jokes.text: Now ask me if I care. / Do you care... / Don't care! I don't care!
jokes.setup: David has been asking Stevie to validate his feelings; he now tries a different tactic.
jokes.punchline: David sets up the 'I don't care' declaration himself, clearly cares deeply, and the timing of the denial — before Stevie finishes the question — reveals the lie.
jokes.explanation: The interruption timing is key — David can't even wait for Stevie to finish asking 'Do you care?' before shouting 'Don't care!' The structure mirrors the joke perfectly: his eagerness to deny caring reveals that he does. Classic David pride comedy.

jokes.text: You are such a sore loser. / I am not! / I'm not. It's just, do you really wanna work for a place that clearly doesn't know what they're doing? / Oh, well, they really want me to work for them, so I have to trust that they do know what they're doing.
jokes.setup: David is rationalizing his rejection; Stevie identifies him as a sore loser.
jokes.punchline: David argues Carol's competence by dismissing the airline; Stevie neatly turns it around — the same airline's judgment that rejected David also chose her.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's logical trap is perfectly constructed — she uses David's own argument against him. If Carol is incompetent for rejecting David, Carol is equally incompetent for accepting Stevie; but Stevie reverses it so that Carol's judgment is sound because Stevie was hired. The economy is excellent and the character dynamics (their competitive friendship) are fully leveraged. One of the episode's best dialogue exchanges.

jokes.text: Admit it. I'm more qualified than you. / Fine. / Admit it. / You're more qualified than me. / Thank you! / Now, let's see what exciting new job opportunities await you in your new career as an airline hostess.
jokes.setup: After the argument, Stevie pushes David to admit she's more qualified.
jokes.punchline: David gives the admission, then immediately reframes Stevie's success as an insult — 'airline hostess' — and reads out embarrassing job listings.
jokes.explanation: David's 'Thank you!' followed immediately by the condescending 'airline hostess' is a great character beat — he can't even enjoy the win before finding a way to take a swipe. The rapid concession-then-pivot is very David. Good rhythm.

jokes.text: Now I would love to use one of Larry Air's 'completely private' new paid toilets. I don't know why they have to stress 'completely private.' / Get out! Get out! / No.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are bickering post-interview; David reads more Larry Air promotional material.
jokes.punchline: The 'completely private' paid toilets — David's mock-confusion about why they'd emphasize that, combined with the ongoing Larry Air budget absurdity.
jokes.explanation: Another Larry Air gag extending the running joke about the airline's minimal standards. 'Completely private' as a selling point for bathrooms is a nice absurdist detail. David's faux-puzzlement about the emphasis is a good character beat.

jokes.text: Ted! Dinner's ready! [calling for the turtle]
jokes.setup: Alexis is home, calling for the turtle she named Ted.
jokes.punchline: Calling a turtle to dinner as if it were a family member — 'Dinner's ready!' — followed by discovering it's gone.
jokes.explanation: The domestic normalcy of calling a pet to dinner, applied to a turtle that is missing, is a sweet absurdist beat. The visual of Alexis calling 'Dinner's ready!' to an empty room (where a turtle was) is funnier than it sounds. Good use of the running gag.

jokes.text: How do I know this isn't like an abduction or something? / It's not an abduction. / You didn't blink. I believe you.
jokes.setup: A driver approaches Alexis to take her to a mystery location arranged by Ted.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's concern about abduction, and her acceptance of a stranger's denial because he 'didn't blink,' is a completely absurd but character-perfect moment.
jokes.explanation: 'You didn't blink. I believe you.' is a gem — it's Alexis's logic for verifying non-abduction, which is both completely wrong and completely her. The specificity of 'didn't blink' as the telltale sign she's trusting is absurdist and quotable. Captures her combination of awareness and obliviousness perfectly. Justified 8.0.

jokes.text: And you're wearing the little tie with your short-sleeved shirt. My little Galapo-guy.
jokes.setup: Ted appears on screen dressed up for their anniversary date.
jokes.punchline: 'Galapo-guy' — Alexis's term of endearment for Ted in the Galapagos — is sweet and funny, especially paired with the image of him dressed up in tropical climate-appropriate formal wear.
jokes.explanation: A warm character moment with a comedic edge. The image of Ted dressed up from the waist up (tie and short-sleeved shirt) while clearly in flip-flops below is the setup for the next joke. 'Galapo-guy' is an Alexis-specific term of affection that's endearing and slightly silly.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's actually really hot here, so the bottom half is just my bathing suit and flip-flops. / Ted! More about your bottom half, please! / Twyla's not standing right behind you, is she?
jokes.setup: Ted is dressed up for their video-call date; he mentions his bottom half is beach casual.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'More about your bottom half, please!' is the obvious flirtatious response; Ted immediately checks if Twyla is listening — she is.
jokes.explanation: Ted's instinct to check if Twyla is watching is a good structural beat, and the confirmation that she is creates a mild cringe moment. The callback to the earlier Ted-and-Alexis video-call intimacy being interrupted by observers threads nicely.

jokes.text: Hi, Ted. / Twyla, again, I can't thank you enough for making this happen. / Um, I can lock up if you wanna go, Twy. / Oh, someone has to serve you dinner. But don't worry, I'll stay out of your way.
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to give Twyla the hint to leave during her romantic video-call date.
jokes.punchline: Twyla refuses to leave because she has to serve them dinner — she will be present throughout the whole romantic date.
jokes.explanation: Twyla's literal-minded 'someone has to serve you dinner' — oblivious to the social awkwardness she's creating — is a good character moment. The joke is in the gap between what Alexis wants (privacy) and what Twyla provides (present but 'out of the way').

jokes.text: In fact, they actually named the new fly 'Alexis.' / The fly that mates with itself? / Yeah, I think the important thing is that now I have a little Alexis here with me, just buzzing around.
jokes.setup: Ted reveals the new fly species they discovered has been named after Alexis.
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately recalls that this is 'the fly that mates with itself' from the opening scene — and Ted deflects with the 'important thing' about having her near.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The callback to the opening scene is perfectly planted and paid off. The structure is excellent: Ted mentions naming the fly after Alexis → Alexis remembers its defining characteristic (it mates with itself) → Ted's deflection ('I think the important thing is...') acknowledges the awkwardness while pivoting to the sweet sentiment. The joke works on comedy (Alexis is named after a self-mating fly), character (Ted's helpless romanticism), and structure (callback from minute 0 paid off at minute 18). Justified 8.4.

jokes.text: Oh, John, hold me. Good is coming, it has to.
jokes.setup: Johnny delivers bad news — the property they wanted to buy was sold.
jokes.punchline: Moira's response is theatrical despair ('hold me') followed by a declaration that sounds like a prayer — 'good is coming, it has to' — which is funnier knowing that Roland is about to burst in with champagne.
jokes.explanation: Moderate Moira moment — her theatricality in crisis is always funny. Gets a rewatch bonus because of what immediately follows.

jokes.text: Oh good, you're lying down! We have big news. / If you're referring to the bath-time video Gwen posted... / N-no, n-no, it's not that exciting.
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn arrive with big news; Johnny assumes it's about Gwen's scandalous video.
jokes.punchline: Roland casually confirms that Gwen's 'bath-time video' exists and circulated — and then downplays it by saying the actual news is 'not that exciting,' implying the video IS exciting.
jokes.explanation: The existence of a 'bath-time video' from Gwen is a darkly funny escalation of her affair with the minister. Roland's 'not that exciting' — meaning the news of the motel investment is less exciting than the scandalous video — is a good accidental joke. Good economy and dark comedy.

jokes.text: Oh well, if that's not cause for alcohol, I don't know what is.
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn reveal they've bought into the motel business.
jokes.punchline: Moira's response to the unexpected business partnership with Roland and Jocelyn is to reach for alcohol — her emotional register for this news.
jokes.explanation: Moira treating the Roland investment as a cause for drinking (not celebration) is a classic character beat — her disdain for the Schitts as business partners, mixed with resigned gratitude. The line is efficient and Moira-specific.

jokes.text: Now, first order of business: we need name tags.
jokes.setup: The motel deal has just been agreed; Johnny is overcome with emotion.
jokes.punchline: Roland's first business priority is name tags — a completely insignificant administrative detail in a moment of high emotion.
jokes.explanation: Three words doing maximum work: 'we need name tags.' Roland's priorities are both immediately wrong and entirely plausible for him. The deflation from Johnny's emotional moment to 'name tags' is the perfect button. Economy score of 9 is justified — it couldn't be funnier with more words.

jokes.text: Why is there a turtle on the floor? / Alexis!
jokes.setup: The emotional motel celebration is interrupted by Roland noticing something on the floor.
jokes.punchline: The turtle Ted — missing all episode — has turned up in the Rose family room; Johnny's exasperated 'Alexis!' closes the episode on the running gag.
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect episode button. The missing turtle — a thread running through the entire episode — resolves in the most unexpected but perfectly logical place: waddling through the family room during a business celebration. 'Why is there a turtle on the floor?' followed by Johnny's single resigned 'Alexis!' closes multiple threads simultaneously (the turtle, Alexis's chaotic life, the family dynamic) with maximum economy. The structure is excellent: the payoff is set up from minute one and delivered at the episode's emotional high point. Justified 8.3.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira endeavor to secure an investor for a new motel while Alexis and Ted struggle with long-distance.
insight: Character-driven cringe sustains a 77-point finale setup despite moderate joke density.
